🟡 Reconnecting Northland

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

Reconnecting Northland supports socio-ecological transformation and community-led regeneration across Northland, New Zealand. It empowers communities through long-term partnerships and localized projects that enhance biodiversity and resilience. They are working across approximately 150,000 hectares of forest, watersheds, rivers, wetlands and farmlands in Northland, with a goal of 800,000 hectares by 2050.

Bioregional Context

Northland (Te Tai Tokerau) forms the narrow peninsula at the northern tip of New Zealand's North Island, surrounded by the Pacific Ocean and Tasman Sea. The region features diverse ecosystems including coastal wetlands, kauri forests, and dune lakes, but has experienced significant habitat fragmentation and biodiversity loss through colonial-era deforestation and agricultural conversion. Despite ecological degradation, Northland retains the highest biodiversity in New Zealand and has the country's highest proportion of Māori population.

Place
Northland, New Zealand
links
https://www.canva.com/design/DAGZcco_p_s/SGmpZwnbMJKz-03C8c8GgQ/edit

Tags

🪢 Network_Weaving 📐 Participatory_Planning_and_Strategies 🏵️ Habitat_Connectivity 📚 Training_and_Employment_Pathways ⛓️ Value_Chain_Development 🪴 Venture_Cultivation 🔀 Grants_Programs ✍🏾 Investment_Vehicles 🔅 Traditional_Ecological_Knowledge 💧 Watershed_Repair

Background

Northland (Te Tai Tokerau) holds Aotearoa New Zealand’s highest native biodiversity, yet decline continues: underfunded volunteer efforts, especially in low-income communities, cannot match the pace of loss. Short, steep rivers could recover quickly if sediment and habitat stress were addressed systemically, but catchments remain degraded.

A large Māori population carries deep whakapapa to whenua—both a demand for justice in resource decisions and a reservoir of kaitiakitanga for regeneration.

Climate shocks and eroding ecosystems now answer decades of underinvestment in care. Central agencies work in silos; electoral cycles chop programmes before they root. Communities often see what is needed yet lack capital, coordination, and time to sustain action.

Origins and Network


Whenua ora, waiora, tangata ora — Land, Water, and People Flourishing Together for the Northland Bioregion of Aotearoa.

This is Reconnecting Northland's vision: a future where thriving ecosystems are the cornerstone of sustainable communities and regenerative local economies. As a Connectivity Catalyst, Reconnecting Northland inspires the connections and weaves the relationships required for community-led socio-ecological regeneration to occur across large landscapes.

They imagine how community environmental efforts can evolve into innovative, connected, resilient, and financially sustainable ventures—capable of working together to heal the social and environmental fragmentation inhibiting Northland's prosperity.

The movement toward sustainable communities follows a structured, phased approach.
1. In Phase 1, they facilitate community visioning and planning by supporting intergenerational dialogue and advising on the Connectivity Action Plan (CAP) process, while helping establish local governance structures.
2. Phase 2 focuses on resourcing and supporting community needs by applying Te Kete Hononga to address training, service access, and capacity building, ultimately growing local capability.
3. In Phase 3, they align funding and investment opportunities with the CAP and foster investment partnerships for local nature-based enterprises.
4. Phase 4 involves the implementation of landscape-scale initiatives, along with monitoring and reporting their impacts.
5. Finally, in Phase 5, they support the development of local regenerative economies, with nature-based enterprises underpinning sustainable community growth.

 🔸

The Connectivity Partnership

Vision and Legacy

Decades of conservation spending have not reversed trendlines; Reconnecting Northland argues for intervening in systems—behaviour, finance, governance—not only isolated projects.

The group works at landscape scale—today roughly 150,000 ha, toward 800,000 ha by 2050—so whenua, wai, and tangata can flourish together.
